result.block: BLOCK A — Architecture spine (packages A1-A5). A1 creates the single pinned vocabulary module lib/engines/domain-types.ts (5 phases DISCOVERY/APPLICATION/QUOTE/PAYMENT/POLICY, 3 subphases DNT/QUESTIONNAIRE/QUOTE_GENERATION, 9 CommitOutcomes, 7 CommitEffects, snake_case ReasonCodes, 3 IdentityTiers), a DomainSnapshot loader (lib/engines/snapshot-loader.ts), and the pure decision core lib/engines/derive-and-expose.ts (derivePhase predicate table + deriveAndExpose exposure with ACTION_RULES), while DELETING both old vocabularies (lib/chat/derive-state.ts 6-phase and lib/chat/phase.ts 3-phase) and re-keying compliance on the new Phase; enforced by enum-closure and vocabulary-closure meta-tests. A2 builds the CommitResult envelope + commit gateway lib/tools/gateway.ts implementing the pinned #8 order (replay-first → legality → HMAC state-fingerprint confirm token → validation → transactional apply under pg_advisory_xact_lock with a CommitLedger row in-tx → post-derive delta = advance_phase), plus tool kind classification (read/commit/internal), materialArgsHash, idempotent replay returning the ORIGINAL envelope, already_applied conflicts, M10 unavailable/pending semantics, and executor routing of every commit through the gateway with the envelope serialized verbatim. CRITICAL BINDING ERRATUM (A2 #0, Ruling 7): task A2.8 consent-truth flip is VOID in Block A — the ConsentEvent model, snapshot consent-source switch, and Customer consent-column drops are owned by package B1; other binding A2 errata require in-lock replay re-check, argsHash-keyed replay lookups, per-tool replay-scope targetRefs keyed on args (not positional state), a ledger row for requires_confirmation attempts, confirm-arg stripping before zod validation (with signDntSchema/acceptQuoteSchema rewritten), and a circuit-state leaf module to break the loader→executor import cycle; addendum A2.ADD-1 mandates a canonical real-DB harness (__tests__/helpers/test-db.ts with DOMAIN_TABLES + resetDb) plus dotenv/serialized-integration vitest wiring (A1 erratum). A3 makes the per-turn LLM tool list EQUAL the engine exposure set (buildTurnTools; DEFAULT_DISCOVERY_TOOLS/lib/chat/default-tools.ts deleted — fixing the live regression where funnel tools were unreachable), executor hard-reject of non-exposed tools, blocked_actions injected into the briefing, per-round exposure re-derivation, GUI action-adapter gateway parity (actor='gui', confirm-token round-trip, no self-confirmed buttons, confirm_required SSE + UI consumer per erratum), the empty-by-design identity-requirements table mechanism, a shared DEGRADED_FLOOR constant, retirement of get_application_status (A3.ADD-1), commit-never-auto-retry policy (A3.ADD-2), and ro+en translations for every ReasonCode (A3.ADD-3). A4 reworks prompt sections per (phase,subphase) under M13's literal criteria: committed inventory doc BEFORE rework, new dntContext/paymentContext/policyContext sections, target sections map (no coaching post-close, workflowInstructions out of ALWAYS), sub-stage briefing facts, pathology scripts 1-4 green before AND after, plus the T4-R6 'save your progress' verification-offer copy (A4.ADD-1). A5 deletes dead config after a mandatory salvage audit: the entire SkillPack subsystem (M12), the Workflow/WorkflowStep/StepTransition/WorkflowSession machine, alwaysAllowed/ALWAYS_ALLOWED_SET/isAlwaysAllowed drift, the stale '25 TOOLS' banner, phantom seed tools, and (A5.ADD-1) the dead profile_extractor/summarizer registrations — enforced by extending the vocabulary-closure meta-test and a bidirectional registry↔ACTION_RULES parity test. Grading note: A5.ADD-1 (delete profile_extractor/summarizer) supersedes A2.2's test that tags them kind:'internal' — final trees should have them gone and the A2.2 test updated; similarly the final tree will contain ConsentEvent via B1 even though A2 must not own it.

lib/tools/gateway.ts exports executeCommit(CommitRequest) implementing the pinned #8 order: actor → replay detection FIRST → legality via deriveAndExpose (blocked reason mapped via outcomeForBlocked: requires_consent/identity/disclosures pass through, temporarily_unavailable→unavailable, else rejected) → confirm token for requiresConfirmation tools (stale/missing → re-issue requires_confirmation envelope with fresh state-fingerprint token, never hard-reject) → validation → $transaction with SELECT pg_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext(conversationId)), in-lock legality re-check, handler run on the tx handle, post-derive: pre/post phase-or-subphase delta pushes 'advance_phase' effect + phaseDelta, ledger row written in the SAME transaction

ERRATUM 2 applied: replay lookup re-run INSIDE the advisory-lock transaction (first read after lock) so two genuinely concurrent identical commits yield exactly 1 idempotencyDisposition:'fresh' applied row — proven by scripts/verify-gateway-concurrency.ts

result.notes: Method: sampled beginning/middle/end of each branch log vs base 9a3d725, tallied conventional-commit prefixes, counted task-ID references and substantive bodies (trailers excluded) across full histories, ran git show --stat on the largest and most sensitive commits, checked feat-commit test pairing (60-commit sample per branch), searched for merges/reverts/duplicate subjects, and used committer-date monotonicity as a force-push proxy. Verified handoff claims against tree contents: fable's quota-blocked F5-partial claim is fully corroborated by docs/superpowers/notes/2026-07-02-zeno-v3-fable-cloud-handoff.md (checklist item 26 PARTIAL + §F5-resume steps); codex's F5-complete claim is plausible — its tracker (docs/superpowers/plans/zeno-v3-codex-progress.md) logs specific run evidence, the legacy-e2e retirement was explicitly permitted by the plan erratum at line 11608, and claimed artifacts (docs/spec-coverage-baseline.json, scripts/diagnose-conversation.ts, sims harness) exist in the tree, though live-run results are inherently unverifiable from git; opus's HEAD is verifiably a docs handoff (CLAUDE.md + .claude/launch.json) and its RESUME banner discloses residual gaps (E2.4-E2.6, spec divergences) even while headlining COMPLETE. Cross-branch constants: all histories are linear (0 merges), 0 reverts, 0 duplicate subjects, 0 force-push artifacts, and ~98-100% of sampled feat commits commit tests atomically with implementation. Shared commit subjects across branches are plan-prescribed, so differentiation rests on bodies, granularity, noise, and handoff honesty — which is where the branches diverge sharply: fable puts WHY in the commits themselves (198/230 substantive bodies), opus splits it between rich fix bodies and 176 tracker chore commits (40% bookkeeping noise), codex externalizes everything to its tracker (1/205 substantive bodies) and ends on an empty-body 23-file dump commit mixing runtime changes into 'chore(release)'.

perBranch.assessment: Best granularity and traceability of the three: 319/435 commits carry plan task IDs, large tasks are decomposed into tracked pieces (e.g. C1.5b pieces 1-5), review findings and verification evidence get their own commits, and the late-stage fix commits have exemplary bodies (root-cause narrative, refuted hypotheses, exact pass counts, browser verification). Ends with an honest, useful docs handoff (0e265bb CLAUDE.md + launch config, verified in tree) after ~30 well-documented post-plan live-debug fixes. Deductions: ~176 chore tracker-update commits (~40% of history) create heavy bookkeeping noise interleaved 1:1 with work commits, ~75% of routine feat commits have no body, and the final RESUME banner's celebratory 'v3 transformation COMPLETE / Blocks A-F all shipped' overstates slightly — the same doc lists plan tasks E2.4-E2.6 as unshipped 'carry-forwards', disclosed but reframed as independent of the plan.

The cleanest process of the three. One commit per task with plan IDs, 198/230 commits carry substantive bodies that explain WHY (root cause, evidence, calibration rationale — e.g. the e2e dotenv diagnosis and the 15-min timeout justification), package boundaries are marked with verification commits citing real pass counts, and zero noise/churn. Standout honesty: the final commit e4076ba declares F5 PARTIAL blocked on OpenAI quota, and the handoff doc verifiably backs it — checklist item 26 explicitly PARTIAL with probe-confirmed quota block, precise done/blocked inventory, and a §F5-resume procedure; earlier honest baselines too ('live sims BLOCKED — no valid LLM keys', 'advance-flow 0/2 = documented pre-A3 regression', owner rulings logged). Minor dings: a few multi-task bundle commits (55e7b7a 'B4 core', 64 files) and task IDs dropped from subjects through blocks C-E (recoverable via the checklist mapping).

Structurally clean history — atomic, well-ordered conventional-commit subjects, tests committed with implementation, per-package verification commits, no churn — but the messages themselves are hollow: only 1/205 commits has a substantive body and only ~55 reference plan task IDs (they fade out mid-branch). All WHY lives in the progress tracker file, which is detailed and honest in nuance (discloses skipped judge rubrics as non-gating, documents red/green evidence per task), and its 'F5 complete' claim is plausible: the e2e retirement was explicitly sanctioned by the plan erratum (line 11608) and claimed artifacts exist in the tree, though live-sim results can't be verified from git. The single worst hygiene artifact in the experiment is its final commit 845e8a6 'chore(release): complete F5 final validation' — an empty-body, 23-file/983-line dump that buries runtime behavior changes (lib/customer/consent.ts, lib/engines/application-rules.ts, lib/engines/derive-and-expose.ts) under a release-chore label at the most audit-sensitive point of the branch.

No green-washing: zero it.skip/it.only/it.todo, zero commented-out tests, zero trivial expect(true) assertions; the only conditional skips are describe.skipIf(!process.env.DATABASE_URL) guards on DB suites.

weaknesses: Spec coverage is 18 of 61 scenarios with 41 tagged @backlog — plan-sanctioned as the truthful initial state and ratcheted against a committed baseline, but the living-spec contract is still mostly IOU; the covered set matches only the plan's designated F1.6-F1.9 translation subset.

Ring separation is by convention, not config: a single vitest.config.ts includes all of __tests__/**, and the skipIf(!DATABASE_URL) guard is inconsistent — only ~20 of 77 integration files have it, so the other 57 (e.g. __tests__/integration/accept-quote-narrow.test.ts, answer-store.test.ts) fail hard rather than skip in a DB-less environment; fails loudly rather than fakes green, but sloppy.

e2e/legacy-retirement.test.ts is the weakest test in the tree: it only asserts three strings exist in e2e/README.md, and vitest.e2e.config.ts now includes ONLY that marker — the 5 legacy LLM-driven e2e scenarios remain in the tree as dead code (retirement was an explicitly plan-offered option, and the replacement sims:spec n-of-m harness plus judge runner exists, but the live gate no longer runs under npm run test:e2e).

The 6 recorded-behavior agent tests (__tests__/spec/agent/recorded-behavior.spec.test.ts) assert over committed export fixtures (2.8MB happy-path.export.json); they verify invariants like no-narration-violations and tool ordering, but their value decays if fixtures are not re-recorded, and nothing in CI forces fixture freshness.

A few peripheral suites are thin: __tests__/performance/bench-pipeline.test.ts leans on 22 toBeDefined checks, and simulation/personas.test.ts has 4 — minor given they are harness/bench scaffolding, not domain logic.

result.standoutObservations: The purity discipline is self-enforcing, not just aspirational: __tests__/lib/engines/vocabulary-closure.test.ts mechanically scans lib/ to prove derive-state.ts/phase.ts are deleted, no second Phase vocabulary exists, retired phase literals are gone, and the SkillPack/Workflow subsystems have zero remaining references — the plan's 'no-second-vocabulary' demand became executable law. Zero `as any` casts across all of lib/ (only ~51 `as unknown as` at Prisma JSON boundaries) is exceptional for a diff of this size (~82k insertions, 205 conventional commits). The gateway's TOCTOU handling is genuinely sophisticated: full pre-checks outside the transaction for fast-path rejects, then pg_advisory_xact_lock plus complete re-derivation and re-check of replay/conflict/exposure inside the transaction. One clever-but-magical choice: withLedgerMetadata attaches commitLedgerId/idempotencyDisposition as non-enumerable Object.defineProperty props so they reach in-process consumers (orchestrator turn-invariants) without polluting the persisted ledger JSON — effective, but implicit in a way a typed wrapper would not be. Most telling architectural flaw found: DomainSnapshot.answers is hardcoded to {} in the production snapshot-loader (line 352) while deriveAndExpose's eligibilityFacts() iterates it — answer-based eligibility facts can only ever fire in test snapshot literals, meaning a declared contract field is silently unwired in prod.
strengths: Exemplary layering exactly as the plan demands: every mandated decision core is a prisma-free pure module — eligibility.ts, suitability.ts, quote-decision.ts (decideQuoteIssue), consequence-planner.ts, accept-quote-legality.ts, payment-schedule.ts, payment-position.ts, policy-machine.ts, provenance-rules.ts, dnt-rules.ts, application-rules.ts, identity-rules.ts, cnp-validation.ts, dependency-graph.ts, quote-lifecycle.ts under lib/engines/. The only prisma-bearing engine files are the designated seams: snapshot-loader.ts (single read boundary), answer-store.ts (the plan's mandated sole Answer-write seam), consequence-applier/runtime (plan executors), and the pre-existing questionnaire-engine the plan said to leave.

deriveAndExpose (lib/engines/derive-and-expose.ts) is a declarative ACTION_RULES table — each tool has exposedWhen + blockedReason predicates over the pure DomainSnapshot; derivePhase is a 10-line predicate table; exposure, identity gating, consent gating, and circuit-breaker blocking compose in one loop. Highly auditable single source of truth for exposure, and the registry explicitly cedes exposure ownership to it (comment at registry.ts:516).

The commit gateway (lib/tools/gateway.ts, 493 lines) is small, decomposed into ~15 single-purpose helpers, and implements the full pipeline: actor permission → ledger replay → strict-conflict detection → exposure check → HMAC confirm-token with state fingerprint → zod validation → advisory-locked transaction with complete re-derivation and re-check inside the lock → commit ledger write with fresh/replay disposition. Handlers receive the transaction via context.db so all writes are atomic with the ledger row.

Payment flip is architecturally clean: accept_quote (quote-handlers.ts) delegates legality to the pure acceptQuoteLegality engine and schedule construction to pure buildSchedule, using CAS updateMany for the ISSUED→ACCEPTED transition; lib/payments/settlement.ts is a proper webhook inbox — unique-constraint-based event dedup (P2002 → replay), amount-mismatch anomaly WorkItems, CAS state transitions, first-capture policy creation inside the transaction, and email/receipt side effects isolated outside the transaction with logged (not silent) failures. Webhook routes are thin adapters over settlePaymentEvent.

Type safety is outstanding: zero `as any` in authored lib code, no ts-ignore, string-literal unions derived from const arrays in domain-types.ts (PHASES, COMMIT_OUTCOMES, REASON_CODES...), zod .strict() schemas for rule sets, narrow `Pick<typeof prisma, ...>` Db types in services (profile-service, work-items/service) enabling transaction injection, and discriminated-union result types (QuoteIssueDecision, QuoteEligibilityGate, SuitabilityGate).

Legacy removal is complete and enforced: derive-state.ts, chat/phase.ts, default-tools.ts, lib/skills/, post-payment.ts, bd-/change-selection-/product-switch-/set-answer-handlers, and skill-pack/workflow seeds are all gone, with the vocabulary-closure meta-test making regressions impossible.

Executor (lib/tools/executor.ts) cleanly separates concerns: commits route to the gateway (validation gateway-owned), reads get timeout + one transient-error retry + circuit breaker + cache; all failures logged with layer/category context, never thrown to the orchestrator.
Commit hygiene: 205 conventional-prefixed commits mapped to plan task IDs (F4.1, D1.2 etc.), making the 26-work-package execution traceable.
weaknesses: Tool-specific knowledge leaks into the generic gateway: resolveTargetRef (gateway.ts:65-105) is a hardcoded per-tool-name switch, and applyBlockedLegalitySideEffects (gateway.ts:137-150) special-cases cancel_quote/quote_expired quote mutation inside the gateway rather than in a per-tool declaration — new commit tools require editing the gateway itself.

DomainSnapshot.answers is never populated by the production loader (snapshot-loader.ts:352 hardcodes `answers: {}` even though answersByCode is computed at line 179-183), yet deriveAndExpose.eligibilityFacts (derive-and-expose.ts:95) iterates it — answer-keyed eligibility rules are dead in the exposure path outside tests; also a vestigial always-empty `dntAnswered` array at snapshot-loader.ts:257.

Parallel unused contracts: gateQuoteEligibility (eligibility.ts:204, self-described 'Final-authority gate contract for D1 generate_quote') and gateSuitability (suitability.ts:85) are exported and tested but never called by production code — generate_quote uses decideQuoteIssue instead, leaving two competing decision contracts in the codebase.

application-handlers.ts (965 lines) contains a ~250-line applyQuestionAnswer function (lines 476-723) mixing validation, flagging, insight bumps, plan application, progress recomputation, and UI payload assembly — the one clear god-function in otherwise well-sized modules; registry.ts grew to 1,475 lines and still hosts two inline handlers (list_products, get_product_info) with ~250 lines of product-shaping helpers that belong in product-handlers.ts.

Handler error handling pattern `catch (error) { return { success: false, error: String(error) } }` (quote-handlers.ts:464, 518, 798 etc.) flattens exceptions to strings with no logging at the handler layer, losing stack traces for anything the gateway wraps as handler_rejected.

Minor convention inconsistency in envelope-returning handlers: payment-handlers' noDueInstallment returns success:false + rejected envelope while scheduleAlreadyCaptured and quote-handlers' rejections return success:true + rejected envelope — behavior is normalized by the gateway/executor, but the mixed success-flag semantics are confusing to readers; small helpers (asReasonCode, rejectedEnvelope, jsonValue) are re-implemented per-file instead of shared.

The 1,567-line chat orchestrator was modified in place (~443 lines changed) but never decomposed, and its pre-existing fail-open compliance catch (complianceResult = passed:true on error, orchestrator.ts:708) was carried forward rather than fixed.

51 `as unknown as` double-casts remain, concentrated at Prisma JSON columns (e.g. flagsForReview casts in application-handlers.ts:551,583, RawProduct cast in registry.ts:375) plus `tx as unknown as typeof prisma` in gateway.ts:437 — pragmatic but unchecked seams where a zod parse would be safer.

result.overallAssessment: The Fable 5 branch is a careful, largely faithful implementation of the plan's hardest mechanisms. I traced the commit gateway (pinned order, per-conversation advisory lock, in-transaction ledger row, idempotent replay of the original stamped envelope, one-shot conflict rejection incl. the D2.5 "targetRef survives ISSUED→ACCEPTED" fix), derivePhase/deriveAndExpose (predicate table matches the plan verbatim; nextBestAction invariant holds because `next` is drawn from availableSet; per-commit pre/post re-derivation), confirm tokens (HMAC over conversationId|tool|argsHash|stateFingerprint — cross-conversation and forged tokens can't pass without the secret; state changes force reissue), money paths (freeze-at-issue via quoteCount/frozenAt, CAS-guarded accept flip, immutable acceptance evidence, integer-minor schedule with last-installment remainder, transactional webhook inbox deduped on PaymentEvent with CAS on Payment, Policy.quoteId @unique preventing duplicate policies, out-of-order failed-after-success handled as no-op), consent (ConsentEvent ledger as sole storage, explicit gdprWithdrawn halt with a re-grant floor), and GDPR export/erasure (all reads/deletes scoped by customerId; no cross-customer leak found; verified-claim merge requires proving channel control). The strongest real defect is a cross-conversation double-charge race in ensure_payment_session: the "single open attempt" guarantee is enforced by the per-conversation advisory lock, but the schedule is customer-scoped and reachable from any conversation, so two concurrent calls from different conversations can each mint a capturable intent. The remaining issues are minor idempotency/replay inconsistencies and one inert anomaly check. No cross-customer data leak, no forgeable token, no double-apply of money commits (CAS + unique constraints hold) were found.

findings.title: ensure_payment_session can mint two capturable payment intents (double-charge) under concurrent cross-conversation calls
findings.detail: The gateway serializes commits only per-conversation: runApplyTransaction takes pg_advisory_xact_lock(hashtext(conversationId)) (gateway.ts:286). But loadLiveSchedule resolves the schedule CUSTOMER-scoped whenever the conversation doesn't point at the accepted application (payment-handlers.ts:40, `customerId: context.customerId` fallback for returning users), and Payment has no uniqueness on (installmentId,status). Scenario: a verified_channel customer with an accepted quote (pending first installment) has two live conversations (e.g. desktop sale + mobile return). Both agents call ensure_payment_session concurrently. They take DIFFERENT advisory locks, both loadLiveSchedule return the same schedule, both deriveSchedulePosition see openAttempt=null (neither's PENDING Payment is committed yet), so both take the recoveryMode!=='resumed' branch and each provider.createPaymentIntent + db.payment.create a PENDING Payment for the SAME installment (payment-handlers.ts:140-158). Two capturable sessions now exist for one installment → the customer/provider can capture both → double charge. The plan explicitly claims this surface is closed ('capturable sessions never stack'); it is closed within one conversation (the lock + resume path) but not across conversations because the lock key and the schedule scope disagree. Sequential cross-conversation is safe (the 2nd sees the 1st's PENDING and resumes); only genuine concurrency stacks.

findings.title: sign_dnt idempotent replay breaks after the commit applies (targetRef keys on activeSessionId which nulls post-sign)
findings.detail: resolveTargetRef for sign_dnt returns `dnt_session:${state.dnt.activeSessionId ?? 'none'}`. sign_dnt is one-shot and NOT replay-exempt, and materialArgsHash folds targetRef into the hash. After a successful sign the source session flips ACTIVE→SIGNED so activeSessionId becomes null: a genuine idempotent resubmit of the SAME sign_dnt (customer/agent re-send after the first fully committed) now computes targetRef `dnt_session:none` and a DIFFERENT argsHash than the stored applied row (which was `dnt_session:S1`). Step-2 replay lookup (by argsHash) misses, the one-shot conflict lookup (by targetRef `dnt_session:none`) misses the stored `dnt_session:S1` row, so the gateway falls through to legality and returns a rejected/blocked envelope instead of replaying the ORIGINAL applied envelope. No data corruption (the legality wall and the handler's own no-active-session guard prevent a second DNT), but it violates the pinned 'idempotent replay returns the original envelope' contract. Note the asymmetry: accept_quote was deliberately fixed for exactly this at line 103 (`state.quote?.id ?? state.acceptedQuote?.id`) so its ref survives the transition; sign_dnt has no equivalent post-sign fallback (e.g. the signed Dnt's sourceSessionId). Concurrent double-submit is still handled correctly (both compute S1 pre-commit and the loser replays inside the lock).

findings.title: acknowledge_disclosures replays a stale envelope and cannot ack a newly-published disclosure version, deadlocking accept_quote
findings.detail: acknowledge_disclosures is neither one-shot nor in REPLAY_EXEMPT, and it takes no material args, so materialArgsHash is constant per quote (targetRef `quote:X`, empty material). The handler (quote-handlers.ts:427) acks exactly the CURRENTLY-missing disclosure docs. Scenario: agent calls acknowledge_disclosures once (acks docs {A}, applied row stored). A new disclosure version B is then published for the product; accept_quote now blocks requires_disclosures because B is unacked. The agent calls acknowledge_disclosures again with the same (empty) args → step-2 replay finds the prior applied row and returns its ORIGINAL envelope verbatim (gateway.ts:186) WITHOUT running the handler, so B is never acked and accept_quote stays permanently blocked. This is precisely the 'a replayed envelope would lie about state' hazard the plan lists as the reason other state-guarded tools (open_dnt_session, generate_quote, select_coverage, ensure_payment_session) were added to REPLAY_EXEMPT; acknowledge_disclosures was omitted. Requires a mid-quote disclosure version bump, so edge-case, but it is a reachable deadlock with no recovery via the tool.

findings.title: Settlement amount_mismatch anomaly check compares our own records, so it can never detect a real provider over/undercharge
findings.detail: The inbox guards `if (payment.amountMinor !== payment.installment.amountMinor)` and flags an amount_mismatch ALERT_FLAG. But Payment.amountMinor is written by us at intent creation equal to the installment amount (payment-handlers.ts:151-156, `amountMinor: pos.nextDue.amountMinor`), and the webhook/settlement path never reads back the amount actually captured at the provider (SettlementEvent carries no amount; Stripe/PayU handleWebhook return only event+providerPaymentId). So the two sides of the comparison are always equal and the branch is effectively dead — a provider that captured a different amount than requested (the exact fraud/reconciliation case this is meant to catch) would settle silently with no anomaly raised. The settlement itself is otherwise correct; this is a missing/ineffective control rather than a wrong state transition.

result.overallAssessment: The commit gateway (pinned #8 order, advisory lock, in-tx ledger, idempotent replay returning the original envelope, conflicting-resubmit rejection), derivePhase/deriveAndExpose predicate table, confirm-token binding (HMAC over conversationId+tool+argsHash+state-fingerprint, consent kept material), ConsentEvent-as-only-truth, GDPR export/erasure scoping, and the webhook settlement inbox (unique [provider,eventId] + PENDING->COMPLETED CAS + amount reconciliation + first-capture policy creation) are, on the whole, carefully and faithfully implemented. However there is one CRITICAL identity-binding vulnerability and several genuine major defects. (1) Identity claim-and-merge treats an unverified declared email/phone mirror as proof of ownership, letting an attacker who merely declares a victim's phone/email absorb the victim's just-verified session — and all repointed conversations/applications/policies/payments — into the attacker's account. (2) write_question_answer/modify_answer replay keys are NOT application-scoped, so a cascade-invalidated (or post-cancel re-application) answer re-given with the same value silently replays and is dropped, deadlocking the questionnaire. (3) The settlement inbox catches ALL insert exceptions as 'replay', so a transient/serialization fault on a real payment_succeeded is acknowledged 200 and never retried — money captured, no policy. (4) ensure_payment_session 'resumed' returns an empty clientSecret, so a returning customer cannot complete a Stripe payment. (5) The R6 'ID document before payment' gate (productDocuments) is a documented no-op in the exposure path, so ensure_payment_session is reachable without the required identity document. The rest of the money/consent/concurrency surface held up under tracing.

findings.title: Identity claim-and-merge trusts an UNVERIFIED mirror -> cross-customer account/data takeover
findings.detail: completeChannelVerification() resolves the 'owner' to merge into by matching Customer.email/phone mirror columns (findFirst where {email|phone: target, id != current, mergedIntoId: null}) with NO check that the owner ever VERIFIED that channel. Those mirror columns are set on a purely DECLARED write: collect_customer_field -> setDeclaredField -> applyWrite writes MIRROR[field] (profile-service.ts:64-74) for email/phone even when unverified, and Customer.phone is not even @unique (schema.prisma:437). Attack: attacker anonymous session ATT calls collect_customer_field field=phone value=<victim number> -> Customer(ATT).phone = victim number. Later the victim (session VIC) does start/confirm_channel_verification for their OWN phone (OTP proves VIC owns it). completeChannelVerification(VIC,'sms',number) finds ATT as 'owner' and runs claimAndMerge(VIC, ATT): every REPOINTER moves VIC's Conversation/Application/Quote/Policy/Payment onto ATT and tombstones VIC. The attacker, controlling ATT, now holds the victim's conversations (PII), applications (CNP), policies and payments. Email works identically (email @unique just guarantees the squatter is the sole match). The merge direction is always 'verified shell INTO whoever holds the mirror', the inverse of what safety requires.

findings.title: write_question_answer/modify_answer replay key omits the application id -> re-answers silently replayed and dropped
findings.detail: resolveTargetRef for write_question_answer/modify_answer returns `app_answer:${questionCode}` with NO application scoping, so argsHash = hash(tool, `app_answer:Q1`, {questionCode:Q1, answer:'no'}) is identical for the SAME question+value regardless of which application it targets. The replay lookup {conversationId, tool, argsHash, outcome:'applied'} (gateway.ts:534) uses a conversationId stable across the flow. Trigger A (single app): the consequence planner INVALIDATEs answer Q1 via a cascade; the customer re-answers Q1 with the same value -> identical argsHash matches the prior applied row -> gateway returns the stored 'applied' envelope WITHOUT running the handler -> Q1 stays invalidated/missing, questionnaire loops. Trigger B: cancel_quote unbinds the app + restores the candidate for a 'prefilled re-application' (quote-handlers.ts:418); set_application creates app2 in the SAME conversation; re-answering identical prior values replays app1's rows -> app2 never completes. dnt_answer (line 219) and select_coverage (line 235) ARE session/app-scoped precisely to avoid this; the app answer keys are the outlier.

findings.title: Settlement inbox catches ALL insert errors as 'replay' -> transient fault drops a captured payment forever
findings.detail: settlePaymentEvent wraps the PaymentEvent inbox insert in try/catch and, on ANY thrown error, returns disposition:'replay' (rolling the tx back with no writes). This is intended for the P2002 duplicate case but catches everything. If the insert throws a non-duplicate transient error (deadlock 40P01, serialization 40001 under concurrent settlement, a connection blip) on a genuine payment_succeeded, the function reports 'replay'; the webhook route logs '-> replay' and returns HTTP 200 (webhooks/stripe/route.ts:100, webhooks/payu/route.ts:95). The provider sees 200 and STOPS retrying, but nothing was settled: Payment stays PENDING, Installment stays PENDING, no Policy is created -- the customer was charged at the provider yet has no policy and the event is never re-delivered. Only the known unique-violation should be treated as replay; everything else should rethrow so the provider retries.

findings.title: ensure_payment_session 'resumed' path returns empty clientSecret -> Stripe payment cannot be completed on resume
findings.detail: On the reuse path (pos.openAttempt && !pos.openAttemptStale) buildResult is called with clientSecret:'' (the comment says 're-issue a session secret so the renderer can mount' but passes ''). show_payment carries the empty secret to PaymentCard, which for providerName==='stripe' mounts <Elements options={{clientSecret:''}}> (components/chat/rich/payment-card.tsx:337-340); Stripe Elements requires a non-empty clientSecret to initialize and renders nothing/errors. Repro (Stripe provider): customer opens payment (mode 'started', real secret), navigates away without paying, returns within the 24h stale window with the PENDING attempt still open -> ensure_payment_session recomputes 'resumed' -> empty clientSecret -> customer cannot pay. The mock provider hides this (no secret needed); a real Stripe deployment cannot resume an open attempt.

findings.title: R6 'ID document before payment' gate is a no-op -> ensure_payment_session reachable without the required document
findings.detail: IDENTITY_REQUIREMENTS.ensure_payment_session = { minTier:'verified_channel', productDocuments:true } (identity-rules.ts:53), and the plan (steps 3129-3131, 3253) requires the document gate to be wired into the exposure predicates + gateway legality so a missing required kind yields requires_identity needs ['document:id_card']. But deriveAndExpose calls checkIdentityRequirement (identity-requirements.ts), which only compares tier + requiredFields/anyDeclaredOf and IGNORES productDocuments (explicit 'Documented no-op here'). The function that DOES handle productDocuments (evaluateIdentityRequirement, identity-rules.ts:67) is never called from production, and the snapshot identity slice never loads validated CustomerDocuments. Net: a customer who has verified any single channel (verified_channel tier) can call ensure_payment_session and pay without ever uploading/validating the product-required ID document -- the R6 compliance gate is silently absent.

findings.title: Per-round exposure refresh skips non-'applied' committing outcomes (e.g. 'referred')
findings.detail: shouldRefreshExposure returns true only when some envelope.outcome === 'applied'. A generate_quote that commits state as 'referred' (App -> REFERRED + frozenAt + WorkItem) is a real committing outcome that changes phase/legality but is not 'applied', so the orchestrator's mid-turn re-derivation (orchestrator.ts:1329) does not fire after it. The model's tool list and exposedTools are not refreshed for the rest of that turn, contradicting the plan's 're-derivation after every commit round'. Low impact because 'referred' is typically turn-terminal, but the invariant is not strictly held.

findings.title: request_cancellation only ever targets the customer's newest policy
findings.detail: requestCancellation (and its confirm predicate at line 156) resolve the policy via findFirst orderBy createdAt desc -- the single most-recent policy. A customer holding more than one policy (e.g. a renewal plus an older active policy) cannot free-look-cancel any policy other than the newest: if the newest is non-ACTIVE it returns illegal_transition while an older ACTIVE in-window policy stays uncancellable via this path. There is no policyId argument to disambiguate. Edge case for the typical single-policy flow, but the free-look right is not addressable per-policy.

label: H2H Package A1+A2: pinned Phase vocabulary,  ranking=['opus', 'fable', 'codex']

result.topic: Package A1+A2: pinned Phase vocabulary, DomainSnapshot loader, derivePhase/deriveAndExpose, and the commit gateway (pinned #8 order, CommitLedger, confirm tokens, idempotent replay)

perBranch.assessment: Most correct under adversarial inputs. Its gateway (lib/tools/gateway.ts, 840 lines) is the only one that structurally guarantees a failing or THROWING commit handler leaves no partial state: a HandlerRejection sentinel rolls the apply tx back and the reject is ledgered exactly once in a separate tx (lines 46-67, 727-748, 804-830), pinned by a dedicated test in __tests__/integration/handler-tx.test.ts — fable/codex commit partial writes alongside the reject row and let handler throws escape unledgered. Also the purest engine (zero clock reads in derive-and-expose.ts; expiry facts pre-computed in the loader), state-derived (unspoofable) targetRefs with per-tool replay/conflict rationale, ledgered requires_confirmation with confirmArgs + an anti-retry-loop message, and the broadest test ring: gateway/idempotency/ledger-id-asymmetry/dynamic-confirm/committing-outcome suites plus __tests__/integration/spec/commit-ring.spec.test.ts asserting phaseDelta APPLICATION→QUOTE on the real gateway (only branch meeting A1 erratum 6's transition-chain ask). Every A1/A2 erratum is addressed (erratum 7 via a documented exception comment at snapshot-loader.ts:93). Costs: the largest, most duplicated gateway (replay logic appears three times across funnel/operator/in-lock paths), a seeded-ledger-row conflict test instead of an end-to-end one, a soft-skip (if n===0 return) in the confirm-path integration test, and integration tests excluded from plain `vitest run` (separate vitest.integration.config.ts, documented).

Best design clarity and the most letter-perfect erratum compliance. The 358-line gateway (lib/tools/gateway.ts) implements the full pinned #8 order in one coherent path: ONE_SHOT + REPLAY_EXEMPT sets with documented per-tool reasoning, in-lock replay+conflict re-check, ledgered requires_confirmation, symmetric ledgerId stamping into stored and returned envelopes, operator actor gate, and lazy quote-expiry normalization. Exposure is factored through pure single-source predicate modules (dnt-rules, application-rules, accept-quote-legality) reused by handlers — the cleanest architecture of the three. Erratum letter-fidelity is exact: engineVersion exported from derive-and-expose.ts with a bump log (erratum 8's specified location), db threaded through resolveGroupCodes (erratum 7 fix option 1), vitest projects split serializing only the integration ring under a single `npx vitest run` (erratum 2's preferred fix), plus a guard refusing to truncate non-test DBs. Weaknesses: a handler returning success:false has its reject row written INSIDE the apply tx (gateway.ts:351-356) so partial handler writes would commit, and a throwing handler escapes unledgered — the plan-draft behavior, but the weakest correctness point vs opus; the engine reads the clock (7 Date uses, recomputing dntExposure repeatedly per derive); sign_dnt post-apply resubmits re-key to a different targetRef so they are engine-rejected rather than replaying the original envelope (deliberate and tested, but a deviation from the plan's replay-verbatim letter); A2 test suites are the leanest of the three, though every erratum-5 binding case is present.

Strongest A2 test suites, muddiest engine. gateway-idempotency.test.ts (251 lines) is the best single test file in the comparison: all erratum-5 binding cases end-to-end, including the interleaved-commit replay that defeats latest-row comparison (erratum 3's exact scenario) and the full requires_confirmation→applied→already_applied ledger sequence driven through a real DNT flow; executor-gateway-routing (105 lines) covers actor attribution. The gateway distinguishes stale_confirm_token from requires_confirmation using the pinned reason code, recomputes targetRef/argsHash inside the lock for consistency, and keeps sign_dnt's targetRef stable so true double-submits genuinely replay the original envelope (closest to the plan's replay letter). But: same handler-failure atomicity gap as fable (reject row committed with partial writes, throws escape unledgered); deriveAndExpose rebuilds the snapshot three times for consent/eligibility/suitability overlays and calls deriveConsents from lib/customer at runtime inside the engine (layering violation); the consent halt runs BEFORE exposedWhen so never-exposed commits surface as consent-blocked noise with imprecise reasons; resolveTargetRef trusts args.quoteId/applicationId over state (conflict-rule evasion surface for an adversarial caller); commitLedgerId/idempotencyDisposition are attached as non-enumerable Object.defineProperty fields (clever but a maintenance trap); the erratum-7 resolveGroupCodes tx-escape is neither fixed nor documented; and fileParallelism:false is applied globally rather than only to the integration ring.

result.rationale: All three branches converged on near-identical A1 cores (pinned vocabulary, plan-verbatim derivePhase, vocabulary-closure meta-test, deleted legacy modules, re-keyed compliance/sections, engineVersion stamps) and all ten A2 errata are substantively addressed everywhere, so the ranking turns on gateway correctness margins, engine purity, and test depth. Opus ranks first because it alone closes the biggest real correctness hole in the package — a commit handler that fails or throws after partial writes: its HandlerRejection rollback + separate-tx audit row preserves both atomicity and the one-row-per-attempt ledger invariant, and it proves this with tests; it also keeps the decision core strictly clock-free and has the widest gateway test ring including real phaseDelta transition-chain coverage. Fable ranks second on the strength of the cleanest, smallest gateway and predicate-module architecture plus letter-exact erratum compliance (engineVersion location, db threading, vitest projects split), despite sharing the handler-atomicity gap and letting the clock into the engine. Codex ranks third — its idempotency/confirm test suites are the best of the three and its replay semantics track the plan letter most closely, but the engine layering (runtime consent derivation inside deriveAndExpose, triple snapshot rebuilds, consent-before-exposure ordering), args-trusted targetRefs, hidden non-enumerable envelope metadata, and the undocumented erratum-7 seam make it the least clean and slightly more fragile under adversarial input. The gap between second and third is small; the gap between first and second rests almost entirely on the atomicity guarantee.

findings.title: Ledger replay aliases distinct logical commits — repeat-value commits are silently swallowed, diverging money truth from the returned envelope
findings.detail: findReplay matches ANY historical applied row for (conversationId, tool, argsHash) with orderBy createdAt ASC — the plan's sketch compared only against the LATEST applied commit. targetRefs also lack entity-instance scoping (resolveTargetRef line 95-97: 'application_answer:<code>' has no applicationId; line 87 'customer_field:<field>'; line 104 default 'conversation:<id>:<tool>' for change_payment_option). Concrete money scenario: customer runs change_payment_option quarterly (applied, schedule A), then semi_annual (applied, schedule B), then quarterly again — the third call hits the FIRST row's hash, replay-first ordering returns the ORIGINAL 'applied, re-rated to quarterly' envelope before the confirm-token step and the handler never runs, so the live PaymentSchedule stays semi_annual (2 installments of annual/2) while the customer is told quarterly; subsequent ensure_payment_session charges the semi-annual amounts. Second scenario: cancel application A, start application B in the same conversation, answer the same question with the same value (e.g. height=180) — write_question_answer replays A's envelope, no Answer row is created for B, missingCodes never shrinks, and every retry with the true value replays again: questionnaire deadlock. Same class hits modify_answer (change 180→185→190→185: DB keeps 190, envelope says 185) and collect_customer_field flip-flops.

findings.title: Snapshot loader surfaces terminal policies customer-wide — after free-look cancellation (or policy expiry) the customer is permanently stuck in POLICY phase and can never quote again
findings.detail: The policy slice falls back to db.policy.findFirst({ where: { customerId } }) with NO status filter, so a CANCELLED/EXPIRED policy is loaded into every snapshot for every conversation forever. derivePhase (derive-and-expose.ts:18) pins policy !== null → POLICY, and generate_quote's exposedWhen requires phase APPLICATION (derive-and-expose.ts:292). Scenario: customer buys, cancels in free-look (policy → CANCELLED, refund executed), then asks to re-purchase: set_application still creates an application, but phase stays POLICY in every new conversation, so generate_quote is blocked forever ('application_frozen'/null reason) and no second sale is possible. The plan's own D4.4 read is 'customer-scoped findFirst NON-TERMINAL policy'; the loader ignores the non-terminal filter.

findings.title: Claim-and-merge splits the live session: the session cookie is never re-mapped to the canonical customer, so post-merge payment/policy tools reject and consent/profile writes land on the tombstoned customer
findings.detail: confirm_channel_verification (identity-handlers.ts:80) merges the current customer A into existing owner B when the verified email/phone already belongs to B: claim-merge repoints the Conversation to B and tombstones A. But nothing follows mergedIntoId anywhere: /api/session keeps returning A from the zeno_session cookie, and the chat route/orchestrator uses client-supplied customerId (orchestrator.ts:831 buildToolContext(state.customerId,...)). Next turn in the same conversation: ensure_payment_session, change_payment_option, get_payment_status, get_policy_info and request_cancellation all check conversation.customerId !== context.customerId (payment-handlers.ts:67 etc.) and now fail with 'conversation_not_found' — the exact returning-customer flow the merge exists for (accept_quote forces verified_channel) dead-ends before payment. Meanwhile withdraw_consent and collect_customer_field write ConsentEvent/profile rows to tombstoned A while the snapshot derives consents from B — consent recorded on the wrong ledger.

findings.title: GDPR erasure gap: never-contracted applications that reached a quote keep all questionnaire Answer rows (health declarations) despite the retention policy saying 'erase'
findings.detail: DATA_CLASSES processes 'applications' (filter { customerId, quote: null }) BEFORE 'quotes' (filter { customerId, policy: null }). A never-contracted customer who generated a quote and then requested erasure: the application is skipped (it has a quote), then the quotes class deletes the quote itself — leaving the Application, its Answer rows (health/personal data), and suitabilityWarningAcks in place forever, violating RETENTION_POLICIES.applications.whenNeverContracted = 'erase'. Additionally no data class ever touches VerificationChallenge, whose 'target' column stores the plaintext email/phone (schema.prisma:460), so PII survives a 'full' erasure.

findings.title: PayU non-COMPLETED IPN is settled as payment_failed — a later COMPLETED event is treated as a replay, the payment sticks FAILED, and ensure_payment_session opens a second charge for money already captured
findings.detail: handleWebhook maps every status other than COMPLETED (PayU sends NEW/PENDING/WAITING_FOR_CONFIRMATION status-change IPNs) to event 'payment_failed'. settlement.ts then CASes the Payment PENDING→FAILED and the Installment→FAILED. When the real COMPLETED IPN arrives, the success path's CAS (payment updateMany where status:'PENDING') matches 0 rows and returns disposition 'replay' — the capture is never recorded, no Policy is created at first capture, and the GET confirm route can't recover (same CAS). ensure_payment_session then sees the FAILED installment, resets it to PENDING and creates a NEW provider intent → customer can be charged twice. The plan's D2 required the webhook inbox to gain an explicit 'ignored' variant; Codex added it only to Stripe's mapper (stripe.ts:144-151), leaving PayU's inherited destructive mapping in a now-sticky settlement pipeline (base code had the same mapping but the flaw was flagged for fixing in this package).

findings.title: Amount-mismatch anomaly detection is dead code in every live path — neither webhook route passes amountMinor into settlement
findings.detail: settlePaymentEvent implements the plan-required amount-mismatch guard (settlement.ts:79: creates a HIGH ALERT_FLAG WorkItem and returns 'anomaly' when event.amountMinor !== payment.amountMinor), but both the Stripe route (line 62-69) and the PayU route (payu/route.ts:85-92) construct SettlementEvent without amountMinor, and the providers' handleWebhook never extracts the captured amount from the event payload. event.amountMinor is always undefined, so the check never fires: a partial/wrong-amount capture at the provider settles as a full COMPLETED payment, marks the installment PAID, and (on first capture) issues the Policy — the plan's D3 'amount mismatch vs the schedule row creates an alert WorkItem' requirement can never trigger in production.

findings.title: Plan-mandated ID-document gate before payment is unenforced: productDocuments is declared but checkIdentityRequirement ignores it and the document-aware evaluator is dead code
findings.detail: identity-rules.ts declares ensure_payment_session/change_payment_option with productDocuments: true, and evaluateIdentityRequirement supports requiredDocs/validatedDocs — but deriveAndExpose uses checkIdentityRequirement, which only checks minTier/requiredFields/anyDeclaredOf and never reads productDocuments; grep shows evaluateIdentityRequirement has zero non-test callers, no Product.verificationRequirements column exists, and no document_review gate guards payment. Scenario: a customer with verified email but no uploaded/validated id_card reaches ensure_payment_session and pays — the plan's B3 seed pins { initiate_payment: ['id_card'] } as a hard KYC requirement, which this build silently drops.

findings.title: generate_quote idempotent replay deliberately disabled — second identical submit returns rejected(application_frozen) instead of the pinned ORIGINAL envelope
findings.detail: NON_REPLAY_TOOLS includes generate_quote, so a duplicate generate_quote (agent retry after a timeout, double-click) never replays: the handler path returns rejectedEnvelope('application_frozen', {quoteId}) (quote-handlers.ts:177-186). The plan pins the opposite semantics twice ('second generate_quote on the same application ⇒ replay of the ORIGINAL envelope, exactly one Quote row' — spec lifecycle/one-application-one-quote, plan line 10460). Codex codified its deviation in __tests__/spec/commit-ring/one-app-one-quote.spec.test.ts (expects 'rejected'/'application_frozen'). Consequence in a real flow: after a network retry the agent receives a rejection, concludes quoting failed, and narrates failure to the customer even though a quote exists — exactly the double-submit confusion the replay rule exists to prevent. Quote-row count stays correct (unique applicationId), so this is a contract/behavior break, not money loss.

findings.title: Provider refunds and intent-cancellations execute inside the advisory-lock DB transaction — a rollback after the external refund enables double refunds on retry
findings.detail: request_cancellation (and cancel_submission in policy-operator-handlers.ts:135) run executeFullRefund within the gateway's prisma.$transaction: provider.refundPayment is called, then payment rows are marked REFUNDED in-tx. If the transaction subsequently aborts (post-derive throw, ledger write failure, serialization error), the DB rolls back to policy ACTIVE / payments COMPLETED while the provider refund already happened; a resubmitted request_cancellation (different fingerprint ⇒ new confirm round, then apply) calls provider.refundPayment again for the same providerPaymentId → double refund at the provider (Stripe refunds.create with no idempotency key, payu refunds POST). Also holds provider network calls under the per-conversation advisory lock for seconds.

findings.title: sign_dnt appends a marketing 'withdrawn' ConsentEvent whenever the marketing answer is absent or non-affirmative — silently revoking previously granted marketing consent
findings.detail: signDnt always writes three consent events, with marketing: marketingGranted ? 'granted' : 'withdrawn'. If the DNT session has no DNT_MARKETING_CONSENT answer (question invisible for the branch, or a renewal session where it wasn't re-asked), marketing is recorded as 'withdrawn'. Since ConsentEvent is the sole consent truth (deriveConsents takes the latest event per kind), a customer who explicitly granted marketing earlier gets it revoked as a side effect of signing an unrelated DNT — re-engagement job then skips them (or, from a compliance angle, the ledger claims a withdrawal the customer never expressed).

findings.title: Round-level exposure refresh only triggers on 'applied' outcomes — 'referred' commits change state without refreshing the tool list
findings.detail: shouldRefreshExposure returns true only if some envelope.outcome === 'applied'. generate_quote's 'referred' outcome mutates the Application to status REFERRED and creates a WorkItem inside the commit, but the orchestrator (orchestrator.ts:1296) skips re-derivation, so the next LLM round in the same turn still sees the pre-referral tool list (e.g. generate_quote still 'available' per the stale turn tools) and the '[State update]' system message is never injected; the model can immediately re-call tools against stale exposure until the next turn. Plan A3 pins 're-derivation after every commit round'.

result.overallAssessment: The Codex build is architecturally faithful to the plan's spine: the commit gateway implements the pinned #8 order (actor → replay-first → legality → confirm token → validation → transactional apply) with a per-conversation pg advisory lock, in-transaction ledger rows, in-lock re-checks of replay/conflict/legality (the plan's known concurrency erratum was applied), HMAC state-fingerprint confirm tokens, and conflicting-resubmit rejection. derivePhase matches the pinned predicate table verbatim, nextBestAction only names available actions, disclosure gating on accept_quote, the narrow CAS ISSUED→ACCEPTED accept with transactional PaymentSchedule (exact minor-unit remainder rule), policy-at-first-capture inside the settlement transaction with a (provider, providerEventId) inbox and Policy.quoteId uniqueness, operator-owned policy transitions, scoped document/export routes, and a real retention-policy-driven erasure executor are all present and mostly correct. The defects found are concentrated at the seams: (1) a critical idempotency design flaw — replay matches the earliest identical (tool, argsHash) row in the whole conversation history with instance-unscoped targetRefs, so legitimate repeat-value commits (change_payment_option flip-flops, re-answering the same value in a new application, modify-back-to-previous-value) are silently swallowed while returning stale 'applied' envelopes, including a money-truth divergence on the payment schedule; (2) the snapshot loader surfaces terminal policies forever, bricking re-quoting after free-look cancellation; (3) claim-and-merge never re-maps the live session to the canonical customer, breaking payment tools and mis-attributing consent right after the forced identity-verification step; (4) GDPR erasure leaves quoted applications' health answers and VerificationChallenge PII behind; (5) the amount-mismatch inbox alert is dead in all live webhook paths; (6) PayU's inherited non-COMPLETED→failed mapping becomes sticky under the new CAS settlement, with a double-charge recovery path; (7) the id_card-before-payment KYC gate is declared but never enforced; and (8) generate_quote replay semantics were deliberately inverted against the pinned spec and codified in the branch's own spec test. No SQL-injection/forgery-grade holes were found in the token, webhook-signature, or export/erasure scoping paths. Overall: a strong, coherent implementation of the engine core with several real, reachable defects in money, identity-merge, and GDPR flows — 6.5/10.

label: H2H Package D2 head-to-head: coupled flip (d ranking=['fable', 'codex', 'opus']

result.topic: Package D2 head-to-head: coupled flip (disclosures, narrow accept_quote, PaymentSchedule, webhook inbox, policy-at-first-payment, conversation terminality)

perBranch.assessment: Highest fidelity and no discovered adversarial hole. All 10 binding errata are visibly applied and cited in code: monthly dropped from INSTALLMENTS_BY_FREQUENCY (lib/engines/payment-schedule.ts:13), pure acceptQuoteLegality exists AND is consumed ONLY inside deriveAndExpose (lib/engines/derive-and-expose.ts:246-253) with the handler explicitly not re-deciding (lib/tools/handlers/quote-handlers.ts:342-380) — the exact erratum-1 shape; sha256('receipt') literal (registry.test.ts:21); PayU env in beforeAll AND signature-check-before-config-read (providers/payu.ts:209-215); mock randomUUID + collision test (providers.test.ts:50). Settlement inbox (lib/payments/settlement.ts) uses a dedup PRE-CHECK instead of create-and-catch, so a transient error propagates → route 500 → provider retries (no silently dropped money event); Policy created PENDING_SUBMISSION + issuedAt inside the same tx; exactly one policy.create in the repo. Uniquely, PayU interim statuses (PENDING/WAITING_FOR_CONFIRMATION) map to 'ignored' (payu.ts:259-266) instead of payment_failed — opus and codex both mark installments FAILED on interim IPNs. Confirm route verifies the provider on BOTH POST and GET, never settles 'pending', 409 on null providerPaymentId. ADD-1 anomalies (bad_signature/amount_mismatch/unmatched) are deduped by an anomalyKey even across FRESH provider event ids, and tested that way (settlement-inbox.test.ts:57-80). Weaknesses: the amount-mismatch check compares Payment.amountMinor vs Installment.amountMinor — nearly unreachable since payments are created from installments, and the provider-captured amount is never reconciled (opus does this better); mismatch is flagged but settlement proceeds. Test suite covers accept blocked outcomes (requires_disclosures/requires_identity), ack replay, inbox dedup, terminality, plus the D2.10 double-delivery sim.

Clean, correct core with the best low-level settlement hygiene but real fidelity misses against the binding errata. Strengths: settlement (lib/payments/settlement.ts) catches ONLY P2002 and rethrows everything else (opus's catch-all can drop events); payment_failed CAS 0-rows → 'replay' (more precise than opus/fable); amount mismatch refuses settlement + creates one ALERT_FLAG; confirm route verifies always, never settles 'pending', 409 on null providerPaymentId — and these are tested at the ROUTE level (settlement-inbox.test.ts:133-154: bad PayU signature through the actual route, confirm 409), which neither other branch does. Registry sanitizes storage-key segments. Fidelity misses: (1) parallel legality — acceptQuoteLegality is imported and called inside the acceptQuote handler (quote-handlers.ts:14, 711-730) while derive-and-expose.ts:296-320 separately re-encodes the same disclosures/identity gates inline, i.e. two-to-three copies of accept legality, exactly what erratum 1 (contradiction #6) forbids; (2) INSTALLMENTS_BY_FREQUENCY keeps monthly:12 (payment-schedule.ts:5) without the erratum-2 alternative (no explicit accept-rejects-monthly test, no config-gated comment) — only zod and product membership guard it, and the PaymentFrequency type admits an unsellable value; (3) PayU handleWebhook reads config BEFORE the signature check (payu.ts:218) — the ordering erratum 4 flagged; (4) PayU interim statuses map to payment_failed, so a PENDING IPN marks Payment+Installment FAILED; (5) the SettlementEvent.amountMinor reconciliation is unreachable from webhooks (WebhookEvent carries no amount). Tests are otherwise on par with the plan spec.

Deepest implementation in places, undone by the one exploitable money hole in the comparison. Strengths: only branch that plumbs the provider-CAPTURED amount (Stripe intent amount → WebhookEvent.amountMinor → settlement) and refuses to settle on mismatch with an in-tx ALERT_FLAG (lib/payments/types.ts:27-31, settlement.ts:111-126) — the most substantive D2.ADD-1; unmatched-payment alerts created inside the inbox tx; legality folded entirely into deriveAndExpose (derive-and-expose.ts:259-265) with gates tested there — a defensible strict reading of erratum 1, though the plan-required pure accept-quote-legality module + test file simply don't exist; excellent comments; settlement-inbox tests include a positive amount-match control. Flaws: (1) GET /api/payments/confirm settles payment_succeeded WITHOUT any provider verification when the customer-controlled `provider` query param is absent, and even when verification runs, a 'pending' provider status falls through to settle (app/api/payments/confirm/route.ts:168-197) — an attacker with a PENDING payment mints a PENDING_SUBMISSION Policy without paying, precisely the paid≠issued class D2 exists to kill (D2.6 said 'provider verification mandatory'); fable and codex both guard pending and verify unconditionally. (2) The inbox insert's bare catch treats ANY create failure as 'replay' (settlement.ts:60-72) → a transient DB error is acked 200 and the provider stops retrying — a droppable money event; codex checks P2002 specifically, fable pre-checks. (3) PayU interim statuses map to payment_failed (payu.ts:282-296), failing installments on interim IPNs. (4) Webhook anomaly alerts aren't deduped across fresh event ids (acknowledged in comments). Everything else — schema, schedule engine (monthly dropped per erratum 2), narrow accept with CAS + one-shot already_applied conflict test, terminality, verify sim, single policy.create — is solid.

result.rationale: All three branches shipped the full D2 surface (schema, registry, disclosures, narrow accept, inbox, provider hardening, terminality, verify sim) with the M9 coupling audit passing (exactly one policy.create, in settlement). The separation comes from the errata discipline and adversarial correctness the plan itself flags as the point of this package. Fable is first: it is the only branch that both applied every binding erratum as written (single legality path through deriveAndExpose, monthly dropped, signature-before-config, anomaly idempotency beyond the spec) and shows no exploitable path — its confirm route always verifies and never settles pending, its inbox never drops or double-applies, and it alone treats PayU interim IPNs as 'ignored' rather than failing installments. Codex is second: its settlement/route hygiene is arguably the best (P2002-specific catch, route-level tests, pending guards), but it ships the exact parallel-legality path erratum 1 forbids (handler calls acceptQuoteLegality while the ACTION_RULE re-encodes the same gates) and keeps monthly:12 without the erratum-2 compensating test/comment — design-drift debts in the decision core, though nothing exploitable. Opus is third despite the richest reconciliation and documentation: its GET confirm route settles unverified (param-gated verification, pending falls through to success), which in a payments package is a policy-minting-without-money bug — the precise contradiction-#5 failure D2 was built to eliminate — compounded by a catch-all inbox insert that can 200-ack and permanently drop a money event on a transient error. Judged on the plan's own priorities (money truth, exactly-once, paid≠issued, one legality computation), fable > codex > opus.

label: H2H C1+C2: dependency graph + consequence pl ranking=['fable', 'codex', 'opus']

result.topic: C1+C2: dependency graph + consequence planner/applier and canonical eligibility module (plan lines 3849-5203)

perBranch.assessment: Highest fidelity: only branch applying every binding erratum — erratum 1 fully (prefixed answer facts + identity age-from-DOB in buildPlannerSnapshot, lib/engines/consequence-applier.ts:88-98), erratum 7 (BD_* CONFIRM_ALWAYS in seed-questions.ts:630-695 + planner first-write test), erratum 10 (flags derived from active revisions with OPEN<->PAUSED recompute inside the applier tx, consequence-applier.ts:150-169, integration-tested pause->correct->unpause), erratum 12 (one_facet_per_commit arg rule, select-coverage-handlers.ts:31-32, tested), erratum 4 fully (contiguity throw + verify-script assert). Single execution path (everything through computeConsequences -> applyConsequencePlan); genuine one-visible-set: snapshot progress and DNT counting both via computeVisibleSet (snapshot-loader.ts:57-74). Receiver-agnostic writer-closure regex with correct allowlist. Excellent decision-traceable docs. Deductions: answer_active_unique lives in the seed bootstrap (prisma/seeds/index.ts:37-39) not a migration; ruleHolds can throw on malformed rule value payloads (zod value is unknown).

Robust and reproducible: only branch with real migrations (20260627130000_c1_answer_revisions creates the partial unique index); most defensive evaluator (eligibility.ts:107-166 — NaN-safe, Array.isArray guards, ''-as-missing, null ruleset tolerated); most principled multi-facet composition via applyPlanToSnapshot threading (select-coverage-handlers.ts:36-51); erratum 7 applied; largest test volume (251-line applier integration incl. confirm-token preview + envelope storage, 363-line cascade verify script). Real defects: writer-closure regex only matches prisma.answer/db.answer while its own lib/gdpr/erasure.ts:186 writes tx.answer.deleteMany outside the allowlist — the meta-test is defeated by its own tree; planner adds a second decision source (graphAllowsAddon edge check beside evaluateEligibility, consequence-planner.ts:174-177) against the one-rule-source canon; gateQuoteEligibility exported+tested but unconsumed in production (quote gating via quote-decision.ts state verdict instead); synthesized non-unique commitIds ('select_coverage:<convId>') break revision->ledger traceability; erratum 4 half (script assert only, no throw); erratum 10 missing (append-only flag writes at application-handlers.ts:551-595); no identity facts in planner eligibility facts.

Unique strengths: only branch calling gateQuoteEligibility inside the real generate_quote host (quote-handlers.ts:160 — the pinned third consumption point); elegant gateway-level dynamic confirmation predicate (answerConfirmPredicate, application-handlers.ts:436-450) with unit test; erratum 4 fully applied; strong gateway integration tests (replay, targetRef collision, uiActions). Serious gaps: NO committed artifact creates the answer_active_unique partial index (schema.prisma:760 has a plain @@index; no migration/script/bootstrap — its progress log admits hand-applied SQL), so the core T6.D2 DB invariant is unreproducible and __tests__/integration/answer-store.test.ts:25-31 fails on a fresh DB. Seeds default EVERY question to CONFIRM_ON_MODIFY with zero CONFIRM_ALWAYS (erratum 7 skipped; DNT_CNP's pinned CONFIRM_ALWAYS lost) while scripts/verify-consequence-cascade.ts legs 7-8 still expect BD first-write confirmation — committed artifacts contradict each other. Snapshot progress uses bd_medical group exclusion instead of computeVisibleSet (snapshot-loader.ts:98-112), reintroducing the dual-visibility-source divergence C1.7 was meant to kill; select-coverage re-implements a partial applier inline and drops statusTransition; buildPlannerSnapshot never supplies identityFacts (erratum 1 half); erratum 10 missing.

result.rationale: All three delivered the full C1+C2 surface (identical pure graph module, edge seeds, single-writer answer store, planner-routed answer/selection commits, legacy-column retirement, typed three-valued eligibility, erratum-2/3/6/8 fixes, verify scripts), so ranking turns on erratum completeness, internal consistency, and reproducibility. Fable is the only branch that is simultaneously plan-complete (every binding erratum including the derived-flags recompute and CONFIRM_ALWAYS sensitivity), single-sourced (one visible set, one execution path), and self-consistent — clear first. Codex is defensive, migration-reproducible, and has the deepest tests, but its writer-closure guarantee is silently void (its own erasure module writes Answer via a tx receiver its regex misses), its planner introduces a second eligibility decision source, its quote gate contract is dead code, and its commitIds break audit linkage — solid second. Opus alone wired the pinned generate_quote gate and has an elegant confirmation design, but judged on committed code it ships a non-reproducible core DB invariant (no artifact creates answer_active_unique, so its own real-DB uniqueness test fails fresh), seeds that contradict its own verify script on BD confirmation, a reintroduced dual visibility source in the snapshot loader, and two skipped errata — third.

result.notes: The Opus 4.8 branch implemented Block A essentially in full, with unusual fidelity to the plan's errata and rulings. Every A1 vocabulary/loader/engine deliverable is present, wired, and meta-test-guarded; the A2 gateway implements the pinned #8 order including all seven binding errata (in-lock replay re-check proven by a live concurrency probe, args-keyed replay scopes, requires_confirmation ledger rows, confirm-arg stripping, circuit-state leaf module) and correctly VOIDED A2.8 (ConsentEvent was built by B1, exactly per Ruling 7 — tracker line 35 documents this explicitly); A3 killed the live tool-exposure regression (advance-flow 0/2 → 2/2 verified), retired DEFAULT_DISCOVERY_TOOLS, and shipped GUI gateway parity with a real confirm-dialog consumer; A4's M13 discipline is exemplary (inventory doc committed before rework, pathology baseline AND after-results with a variance investigation); A5 deleted SkillPack + Workflow end-to-end behind a committed salvage audit and added the bidirectional registry⇔ACTION_RULES guard, plus a beyond-plan self-tuning-apparatus deletion under a recorded user ruling. result.topic: F1+F5: BDD gherkin traceability harness, scenario translation, agent assertion layer, final validation gauntlet

perBranch.assessment: Most complete and most hardened harness. Parser goes beyond the plan (joinWrappedSteps for soft-wrapped step lines, orphan-table-row detection in lib/spec/parse-workflow-feature.ts). The scanner (lib/spec/registry.ts) closes errata #1 with an allow-list (*.spec.test.ts only), comment stripping, and skip/todo-wrapper rejection — each with dedicated adversarial tests in __tests__/spec/registry.test.ts. Traceability (traceability.meta.test.ts) is stricter than the plan: pinned 2-id @agent-judge exemption list, per-row in-range #exN outline coverage (bare id cannot cover a multi-row outline), row-index orphan validation, and a covered metric that excludes backlog/judge. Unique operations-map.meta.test.ts welds every map target to the shipped tool registry — codex has nothing like it and its map points at 4 nonexistent tools. Errata #3 fully implemented (@judge: secondary tags, 6 rubrics, closure keyed on tags). Most translations: 28/61 covered, 31 backlog (best of the three); 25 engine registrations incl. all 5 modify-answer rows. conversation-assertions.ts has the only correctly per-turn-joined assertNoBlockedActionExecuted, with a regression test for the cross-turn false positive fable/codex are still exposed to. run-spec-sims.ts is a real 357-line runner with SSE ui_action capture and a genuine GUI sign_dnt confirm-token round-trip; the harness surfaced a real agent bug (DNT question-code hallucination + a tx-visibility engine bug) that was then fixed in the owning surfaces — exactly the plan's intent. Deductions: the F5.5 discovery→policy live sim was substituted by a deterministic integration chain (happy-path sim stops at DNT SIGNED; no payment/policy world hooks), the quote-decline scenario was dropped, and the accept_quote idempotency scenario was sidestepped in favor of generate_quote in commit-ring.spec.test.ts. All honestly documented in zeno-v3-progress.md.

Faithful, well-documented implementation with the best F5.5 design but incomplete live evidence. Files are close to plan-verbatim with every deviation recorded in comments (taxonomy.ts, spec-snapshots.ts reusing the engine ring's real factory). Errata #3 fully implemented: 6 rubrics with real scenario specIds, closure enforced both directions in taxonomy-closure.meta.test.ts (rubrics↔@judge tags 1:1 AND every @agent-judge carries a @judge tag). Best commit-ring fidelity to the plan's mechanics (__tests__/integration/spec-commit-ring.test.ts): accept_quote idempotency with a real requires_confirmation confirmToken preflight (errata #7), conflicting-resubmit rejection, #ex4 cascade with Postgres-level clearing + ledger effects, advisory-lock concurrency; DB safety via a serialized integration project + env aliasing + a 'test'-URL truncate guard (errata #8). F5.5 machinery is the most honest of the three: worldHooks in run-spec-sims.ts only play the outside world (consume a real verification challenge, GUI doc upload, provider settlement) so the agent must drive generate_quote/accept_quote itself, plus full DB evidence checks (Application COMPLETED, Quote ACCEPTED, first installment PAID, Policy PENDING_SUBMISSION, accept_quote advance_phase). Weaknesses: traceability.meta.test.ts is plan-verbatim (bare id credits a whole outline; no judge-exemption pinning; covered counts judge/backlog scenarios), the spec-scan-ignore pragma is a blunt opt-out any file could invoke, only 21/61 covered (38 backlog), the F5 live gates were never run (handoff note: blocked on OpenAI quota — honest but incomplete), and the committed happy-path fixture shows the agent spamming sign_dnt 40 times while stuck in DISCOVERY, so no in-repo evidence of a full-funnel pass exists.

Clean, competent core with the best commit-ring assertions but real fidelity and correctness losses in the judge layer and the F5.5 sim. Strengths: __tests__/spec/commit-ring/* is the most detailed of the three (cascade-invalidate asserts INVALIDATED rows with causedByKey/invalidatedReason causality, confirmation preview→confirm flows, concurrency checks both actors' ledger rows); backlog-ratchet.meta.test.ts is the strongest ratchet (id-level check prevents backlog swapping, not just count growth); errata #1 solved by constructing registry.test.ts fixtures so the scan regex cannot match, plus invalid-id filtering; F5 e2e errata explicitly resolved (e2e/README.md + legacy-retirement.test.ts); global DATABASE_URL→zeno_codex_test remap plus a 'test'-URL guard. Weaknesses: errata #3 only half-done — lib/testing/judge/rubrics.ts keeps just 2 of the 6 required rubrics, the 4 remaining @judge: tags in the .feature are enforced by nothing (taxonomy-closure checks only @agent-judge↔rubrics), so the plan's judge quarantine for the irreducibly-linguistic set is mostly dropped; run-judge.ts has a matching bug (looks for sim exports containing the specId slug, but exports are named happy-path/dnt-refusal/quote-decline) so every verdict is permanently SKIPPED — their own F5 notes observed this and left it; operations-map.ts self-maps identify_customer/get_policy_status/get_policy_documents/get_product_addon_info to tools absent from its own lib/tools/registry.ts, with no enforcement test; the F5.5 'policyReached' happy path is rigged — scripts/sims/funnel.ts, selection.ts, confirmations.ts and profile.ts have the harness execute select_coverage, generate_quote, acknowledge_disclosures, accept_quote and ensure_payment_session directly through the gateway and fabricate a consumed VerificationChallenge with a fake codeHash, so reaching POLICY proves the gateway (already proven by commit-ring), not the agent — defeating the deadlock-class signal F5.5 exists to catch; the committed happy-path fixture only reaches APPLICATION; fewest translations (18/61 covered, 41 backlog).

result.rationale: All three shipped the full F1 skeleton (parser, spec()/scanner registry, operations map with errata-#5 payment mapping, tagging meta-tests, traceability, taxonomy closure, rubrics, engine + commit-ring translations, assertion library, real sim runners, judge runner, ratchet + committed baseline) — partly because the delivered .feature arrived pre-tagged with @id/class/@judge tags. They separate on hardening, errata compliance, translation volume, and the integrity of the F5.5 evidence. Opus wins decisively: it is the only branch that adversarially hardened the enforcement loop (allow-list scanner with comment/skip rejection, pinned judge exemptions, per-row outline coverage, orphan row-range checks, an operations-map↔registry weld test), translated the most scenarios (28 covered vs 21/18), fixed the known false-positive in the blocked-action assert, and used the harness to find and fix a real agent/engine bug — the plan's stated purpose. Its one material gap (no live discovery→policy sim; deterministic-chain substitute) is documented honestly. Fable edges codex for second: its F1 is more complete (6-rubric registry with bidirectional @judge closure vs codex's 2 rubrics and 4 dead tags), its operations map is reconciled to shipped tools, its commit-ring matches the plan's accept_quote/confirmToken mechanics, and its F5.5 world-hook design keeps the agent in the driver's seat — but its live F5 run was quota-blocked and its committed happy-path fixture records a stuck sign_dnt loop. Codex has the best commit-ring test detail and the strongest ratchet, yet loses points for a rigged happy-path sim in which the harness itself commits the quote/payment funnel through the gateway and fabricates channel verification (making 'policyReached' near-vacuous as agent evidence), a judge runner that can never find an export to judge, a partly fictional operations map, and the lowest translation coverage. Judged on code, the order is opus, fable, codex.
